content:
# Philemon Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 223-224 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHM_001_page_001.jpg, PHM_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paulos, a prisoner of Christ Iesous, and Timotheos the brother, to Philemon the beloved and our fellow-worker,
2. and to Apphia the sister, and to Archippos our fellow-soldier, and to the assembly at your house:
3. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and [the] Lord Iesous Christ.
4. I give-thanks to my God always, making mention of you upon my prayers,
5. hearing of your love and the faith which you have toward the Lord Iesous and unto all the holy-ones,
6. so-that the fellowship of your faith might become effective in [the] knowledge of every good [thing] which [is] in us unto Christ,
7. For I had much joy and comfort upon your love, because the inward-parts of the holy-ones have been refreshed through you, brother.
8. Therefore, having much boldness in Christ to command you the [thing which is] fitting,
9. on-account-of love rather I appeal, being such-a-one as Paulos, an old-man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Iesous —
10. I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in the bonds, Onesimos,
11. the [one] once useless to you, but now useful to you and to me,
12. whom I sent-back to you, him, that is, my own inward-parts;
13. whom I was wishing to retain with myself, so-that on your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the good-news,
14. but apart-from your purpose I wished to do nothing, so-that your good [deed] might not be as according-to compulsion but according-to willingness.
15. For perhaps on-account-of this he was separated [from you] for an hour, so-that you might have-him-back forever,
16. no longer as a slave, but above a slave, a brother beloved, especially to me, and how much more to you, both in [the] flesh and in [the] Lord.
17. If therefore you hold me [as] a partner, receive him as me.
18. But if he wronged you [in] anything or owes [anything], charge this to me.
19. I, Paulos, wrote with my own hand: I will repay — in-order-that I may not say to you that you also owe me yourself besides.
20. Yes, brother, may I have profit from you in [the] Lord; refresh my inward-parts in Christ.
21. Having been persuaded of your obedience, I wrote to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say.
22. And at-the-same-time also prepare for me a lodging, for I hope that through your prayers I shall be granted to you.
23. Epaphras greets you, my fellow-captive in Christ Iesous,
24. Markos, Aristarchos, Demas, Loukas, my fellow-workers.
25. The grace of the Lord Iesous Christ [be] with your spirit. Amen.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

**Manuscript and Folio:** Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE), quire 77. Page 223 (column 4) contains Philemon 1-9; page 224 (columns 1-2) contains Philemon 10-25. The text concludes with AMHN and the remainder of page 224 is blank (end of quire).

**Verse 2 — APPHIA:** Sinaiticus reads ΑΦΦΙΑ (Apphia) with the designation "the sister" (τη αδελφη). Some later manuscripts read "the beloved" (τη αγαπητη) instead. The EMET Bible follows Sinaiticus here.

**Verse 6 — "in us" vs. "in you":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΗΜΙΝ ("in us"), whereas many later manuscripts read ΕΝ ΥΜΙΝ ("in you"). This is a significant variant. The EMET Bible follows the Sinaiticus reading.

**Verse 7 — "joy" (ΧΑΡΑΝ):** Sinaiticus reads ΧΑΡΑΝ ("joy"). Some manuscripts read ΧΑΡΙΝ ("grace/gratitude") here. The EMET Bible follows Sinaiticus.

**Verse 9 — ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΗΣ ("old-man"):** Sinaiticus clearly reads ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΗΣ (presbytes, "old man"). Some scholars have proposed an emendation to ΠΡΕΣΒΕΥΤΗΣ (presbeutēs, "ambassador"), but this has no manuscript support in Sinaiticus. The EMET Bible translates what is written.

**Verse 10 — ΟΝΗΣΙΜΟΝ:** The name Onesimos (Ονησιμος) means "useful/profitable" in Greek, creating a deliberate wordplay with verse 11 (ΑΧΡΗΣΤΟΝ/ΕΥΧΡΗΣΤΟΝ, "useless/useful") and verse 20 (ΟΝΑΙΜΗΝ, "may I have profit"). This paronomasia is present in the source text but largely lost in English.

**Verse 12 — Sinaiticus text:** The manuscript reads the shorter text here; some later witnesses add "and you, receive him" (συ δε αυτον προσλαβου). Sinaiticus does not include this addition.

**Verse 20 — ΟΝΑΙΜΗΝ:** This is an optative form of ΟΝΙΝΗΜΙ ("to profit/benefit"), itself a play on the name Onesimos. Translated as "may I have profit" to preserve the verbal connection.

**Verse 25 — "Lord Iesous Christ":** Sinaiticus reads the full form ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ. Some manuscripts omit ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ. Sinaiticus also concludes with ΑΜΗΝ, which some manuscripts lack.

**General Notes on Sinaiticus Orthography:** The manuscript exhibits typical Sinaiticus features including occasional itacistic spellings and nomina sacra abbreviations (ΧΥ for ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ, ΙΥ for ΙΗΣΟΥ, ΚΥ for ΚΥΡΙΟΥ, ΘΥ for ΘΕΟΥ). These have been expanded silently in the translation.

**Translation Philosophy:** Every word rendered here follows the Greek word order and morphology as closely as English permits. Hyphenated compounds represent single Greek words. Brackets indicate words implied by Greek grammar but absent from the text. No smoothing or paraphrase has been applied.

-- EMET
